entries_to_update.change_rationale: Updated description, likely_cause, how_it_appears_in_play, detection_signal, best_feedback_response, best_clean_replay_task, and reflection_prompt to cover the new cross-unit label confusion risk introduced by the mixed truck-and-helicopter pool, where learners cannot reliably distinguish which capacity label belongs to which unit type.

best_clean_replay_task.old: Text-only mode: replace truck icons with plain cards labeled 'Water: 3', 'Water: 1', 'Water: 2'. Demand card labeled 'Fire needs: 4'. Player matches values without icon distraction.
best_clean_replay_task.new: Text-only mode: replace truck and helicopter icons with clearly color-coded plain cards — blue cards labeled 'Truck Water: 3', 'Truck Water: 1' and orange cards labeled 'Heli Water: 2', 'Heli Water: 4'. Demand card labeled 'Fire needs: 4'. Player matches values using only one color group at a time, then mixed, to practice anchoring label to unit type before reintroducing icons.

how_it_appears_in_play.old: Player consistently selects the highest-numbered trucks first regardless of demand. Or selects the lowest-numbered trucks as 'fastest'. Or ignores the capacity label and picks by visual truck size.
how_it_appears_in_play.new: Player selects a truck-helicopter combination whose combined capacity appears to meet demand but whose individual labels were cross-read (e.g., reads helicopter capacity as the truck's value and vice versa, arriving at a wrong sum). Player may also dispatch a helicopter to an urban fire believing its capacity label applies the same way as a truck's, or ignore one unit type's labels entirely and rely only on the other type's numbers. Alternatively, player consistently selects the highest-numbered units first regardless of demand or terrain, as in the original pattern.

reflection_prompt.old: What did you think the number on the truck meant? What does it actually mean?
reflection_prompt.new: When you looked at the trucks and helicopters together, could you tell which capacity number belonged to which unit? How did you decide which numbers to add up?

best_feedback_response.old: The number on the truck shows how much water it carries — not how fast or how important it is. You need those numbers to add up to the fire size.
best_feedback_response.new: Each unit has its own capacity number — trucks and helicopters have separate labels. Make sure you're reading the number that belongs to the unit you're selecting. The numbers need to add up to the fire size, using only the units you actually dispatch.

detection_signal.old: Truck selection order correlates with capacity rank (always high-first or always low-first) across ≥ 4 consecutive orders regardless of demand value.
detection_signal.new: Dispatch combinations where the selected truck(s) and helicopter(s) have swapped capacity assumptions — i.e., actual combined truck capacity does not meet demand but would if helicopter values were substituted in, or wrong-terrain units are selected with correct-looking capacity sums. Also flagged: truck selection order correlates with capacity rank (always high-first or always low-first) across ≥ 4 consecutive orders regardless of demand value.

likely_cause.old: In strategy and simulation games, numbers on units often represent attack power, speed, or priority. The semantic of 'capacity' requires explicit instruction. Icon design that resembles a rank or star rating increases the risk of this misread.
likely_cause.new: When two distinct unit types (trucks and helicopters) share the same visual format for their capacity label, learners cannot reliably anchor a number to its source unit. In strategy and simulation games, numbers on units often represent attack power, speed, or priority, compounding the misread. Icon proximity, overlapping layouts, or similar badge designs increase the risk that a learner reads a helicopter's capacity value while intending to evaluate a truck, or pools both values incorrectly.

description.old: Learner interprets the capacity number on the truck as a priority indicator, speed rating, or distance rank — not as the volume of water the truck carries. Selects trucks by perceived urgency rather than by capacity value.
description.new: Learner fails to distinguish between truck capacity labels and helicopter capacity labels, treating the capacity number as a shared pool or misattributing a helicopter's capacity value to a selected truck (or vice versa). May also interpret capacity numbers as priority, speed, or distance rank rather than water volume. Selects resources by perceived urgency or by confusing which label belongs to which unit type.

revised_entry.label: Misreads capacity icon as priority or speed
revised_entry.description: Learner fails to distinguish between truck capacity labels and helicopter capacity labels, treating the capacity number as a shared pool or misattributing a helicopter's capacity value to a selected truck (or vice versa). May also interpret capacity numbers as priority, speed, or distance rank rather than water volume. Selects resources by perceived urgency or by confusing which label belongs to which unit type.

In strategy and simulation games, numbers on units often represent attack power, speed, or priority, compounding the misread. Icon proximity, overlapping layouts, or similar badge designs increase the risk that a learner reads a helicopter's capacity value while intending to evaluate a truck, or pools both values incorrectly.

Player may also dispatch a helicopter to an urban fire believing its capacity label applies the same way as a truck's, or ignore one unit type's labels entirely and rely only on the other type's numbers. Alternatively, player consistently selects the highest-numbered units first regardless of demand or terrain, as in the original pattern.

Also flagged: truck selection order correlates with capacity rank (always high-first or always low-first) across ≥ 4 consecutive orders regardless of demand value.

Demand card labeled 'Fire needs: 4'. Player matches values using only one color group at a time, then mixed, to practice anchoring label to unit type before reintroducing icons.

entries_to_update.change_rationale: Updated description, likely_cause, how_it_appears_in_play, detection_signal, best_feedback_response, best_clean_replay_task, and reflection_prompt to capture the terrain-restriction confusion risk introduced in the revised brief, where dispatching the wrong resource type to the wrong terrain is now a primary fail state distinct from the original reuse/capacity-only misconception.

best_clean_replay_task.old: Tutorial: yard of 3 trucks. Demand = 1. Player sends 1 truck. Yard visually updates to show 2 trucks remaining. Second demand appears — player must choose from the remaining 2 only. Explicit 'truck departed' animation.
best_clean_replay_task.new: Tutorial: one urban fire (severity 3) and one forest fire (severity 3). Pool shows one truck (capacity 3) and one helicopter (capacity 3), both clearly labeled with terrain icons. Player must dispatch the truck to the urban fire and the helicopter to the forest fire. Attempting the wrong pairing triggers an animated 'blocked route' visual before the player can confirm, then prompts them to reconsider.

how_it_appears_in_play.old: Repeated taps on an empty yard slot or on a truck already selected. Visible confusion when yard updates. Performance drops specifically after the first multi-truck dispatch. Player tries to undo a selection by tapping again.
how_it_appears_in_play.new: Player selects a truck for a forest-tagged fire or a helicopter for an urban-tagged fire and confirms dispatch, receiving a 'wasted dispatch' failure. Player expresses surprise when the resource fails to extinguish the fire despite sufficient capacity. Player may also attempt to select a resource still shown in cooldown state, expecting it to be usable.

reflection_prompt.old: After you sent a truck, did you expect it to come back? How did the yard change?
reflection_prompt.new: Before you picked a resource, did you check the fire's terrain tag? What happens when you send a truck to a forest fire instead of a helicopter?

best_feedback_response.old: Each truck goes to one fire and stays there. Once it leaves the yard, it's gone for this round.
best_feedback_response.new: That resource can't reach that fire. Trucks fight urban fires; helicopters fight forest fires. Check the terrain tag before you pick your resources.

detection_signal.old: Tap event on an already-selected or already-dispatched truck position within the same incident. Occurs ≥ 2 times per session.
detection_signal.new: Dispatch event where the selected resource type does not match the fire's terrain tag (truck→forest or helicopter→urban). Also: tap event on a resource currently in cooldown state. Either event occurring ≥ 2 times per session.

likely_cause.old: In most resource contexts, items are persistent or rechargeable. The single-use-per-dispatch constraint is a mathematical rule that limits the solution space — it is the core of the subset-sum mechanic. Without clear visual feedback of truck departure, learners assume trucks are always available.
likely_cause.new: In most resource contexts, capacity is the only relevant constraint for matching supply to demand. The terrain-type restriction is a secondary rule that filters the valid subset before capacity is even considered. Without salient visual differentiation between terrain tags and resource icons, learners collapse the two-dimensional constraint (terrain + capacity) into a one-dimensional one (capacity only). Cooldown visibility also creates ambiguity about when a resource is truly ready.

description.old: Learner believes trucks return to the yard after dispatch and can be selected again for the same incident or the next one. Attempts to select a truck that has already left the yard.
description.new: Learner ignores terrain restrictions and dispatches a fire truck to a forest fire or a helicopter to an urban fire, believing any resource with sufficient capacity can respond to any fire. Additionally, learner may not fully grasp the cooldown mechanic and treat resources on cooldown as immediately available.

revised_entry.label: Tries to reuse a dispatched truck
revised_entry.description: Learner ignores terrain restrictions and dispatches a fire truck to a forest fire or a helicopter to an urban fire, believing any resource with sufficient capacity can respond to any fire. Additionally, learner may not fully grasp the cooldown mechanic and treat resources on cooldown as immediately available.
revised_entry.likely_cause: In most resource contexts, capacity is the only relevant constraint for matching supply to demand. The terrain-type restriction is a secondary rule that filters the valid subset before capacity is even considered. Without salient visual differentiation between terrain tags and resource icons, learners collapse the two-dimensional constraint (terrain + capacity) into a one-dimensional one (capacity only). Cooldown visibility also creates ambiguity about when a resource is truly ready.
revised_entry.how_it_appears_in_play: Player selects a truck for a forest-tagged fire or a helicopter for an urban-tagged fire and confirms dispatch, receiving a 'wasted dispatch' failure. Player expresses surprise when the resource fails to extinguish the fire despite sufficient capacity. Player may also attempt to select a resource still shown in cooldown state, expecting it to be usable.
revised_entry.detection_signal: Dispatch event where the selected resource type does not match the fire's terrain tag (truck→forest or helicopter→urban). Also: tap event on a resource currently in cooldown state. Either event occurring ≥ 2 times per session.
revised_entry.best_feedback_response: That resource can't reach that fire. Trucks fight urban fires; helicopters fight forest fires. Check the terrain tag before you pick your resources.
revised_entry.best_clean_replay_task: Tutorial: one urban fire (severity 3) and one forest fire (severity 3). Pool shows one truck (capacity 3) and one helicopter (capacity 3), both clearly labeled with terrain icons. Player must dispatch the truck to the urban fire and the helicopter to the forest fire. Attempting the wrong pairing triggers an animated 'blocked route' visual before the player can confirm, then prompts them to reconsider.
revised_entry.reflection_prompt: Before you picked a resource, did you check the fire's terrain tag? What happens when you send a truck to a forest fire instead of a helicopter?

entries_to_update.change_rationale: Updated description, likely_cause, how_it_appears_in_play, detection_signal, best_feedback_response, best_clean_replay_task, and reflection_prompt to reflect the expanded three-attribute constraint set (terrain + capacity + cooldown) introduced by the revised loop brief, replacing the original two-attribute (capacity + urgency) framing throughout.

best_clean_replay_task.old: Remove urgency attribute entirely. Capacity-only mode for 3 orders. Then reintroduce urgency with explicit 'first match size, then check flag' instruction and a step-indicator in the UI.
best_clean_replay_task.new: Run a single-constraint drill sequence: (1) terrain-only matching for 2 orders with capacity and cooldown hidden, (2) add capacity matching for 2 orders with cooldown hidden, (3) reintroduce cooldown indicators with an explicit step-by-step checklist overlay (terrain → capacity → cooldown) visible in the UI before each dispatch.

how_it_appears_in_play.old: Correct capacity matching but consistent urgency-bonus misses. Or urgency recognized but capacity now incorrect. Performance degrades specifically when high-urgency incidents appear. 'Close' selections that satisfy one constraint but not the other.
how_it_appears_in_play.new: Player selects resources that meet capacity and cooldown constraints but picks the wrong terrain type (e.g., sends a truck to a forest fire). Or selects the correct terrain type and capacity but inadvertently includes a resource still on cooldown, leaving actual dispatch capacity below threshold. Performance degrades specifically when two or more constraints are stressed simultaneously — such as a forest fire with high severity appearing shortly after helicopters entered cooldown. 'Close' selections that satisfy two of the three constraints but fail on the third are the signature pattern.

reflection_prompt.old: When you were picking trucks, what did you think about first — the size of the fire or how urgent it was? What order would work better?
reflection_prompt.new: When you were picking trucks and helicopters, what did you think about first — the type of terrain, how much water you needed, or which vehicles were available? What order would make it easiest to check all three things?

best_feedback_response.old: First match the fire size, then check urgency for the bonus. Do one at a time.
best_feedback_response.new: Check three things in order: first, does the terrain match? Then, do the capacity numbers add up? Finally, are those resources off cooldown? One step at a time.

detection_signal.old: Capacity match correct in ≥ 70% of orders but urgency-optimal selection made in ≤ 30% of those same orders. Degraded overall accuracy on high-urgency incidents specifically.
detection_signal.new: Terrain-correct selections in ≥ 65% of dispatches AND capacity-correct selections in ≥ 65% of dispatches, but both constraints satisfied together in ≤ 35% of dispatches. Separately, cooldown-violation rate (selecting a resource still in cooldown) spikes to ≥ 40% on rounds immediately following a high-severity incident. Errors cluster on incidents where at least two constraints are non-trivial simultaneously.

label.old: Cannot track capacity and urgency simultaneously
label.new: Cannot track terrain + capacity + cooldown simultaneously

likely_cause.old: Two-attribute constraint reasoning requires holding two independent criteria and integrating them into a single selection decision. This is a genuine cognitive load increase — not a skill gap, but a capacity gap. Common at the transition from L1 (one attribute) to L2+ (two attributes).
likely_cause.new: Three-attribute constraint reasoning requires simultaneously filtering by terrain type, summing capacity values to hit an exact target, and excluding resources on cooldown. Each additional constraint multiplicatively increases working memory demand. This is a cognitive load ceiling issue rather than a conceptual misunderstanding of any individual rule. Most pronounced when a high-severity or time-pressured incident forces rapid decisions across all three dimensions at once.

description.old: Learner handles capacity matching correctly when urgency is ignored but cannot hold both capacity and urgency constraints in working memory simultaneously. Optimizes one attribute while ignoring the other.
description.new: Learner may handle one or two of the three active constraints (terrain compatibility, combined capacity, cooldown availability) correctly in isolation but cannot integrate all three into a single dispatch decision. Optimizes for one or two attributes while ignoring the third, producing dispatches that are partially correct but ultimately invalid or suboptimal.

revised_entry.label: Cannot track terrain + capacity + cooldown simultaneously
revised_entry.description: Learner may handle one or two of the three active constraints (terrain compatibility, combined capacity, cooldown availability) correctly in isolation but cannot integrate all three into a single dispatch decision. Optimizes for one or two attributes while ignoring the third, producing dispatches that are partially correct but ultimately invalid or suboptimal.

Most pronounced when a high-severity or time-pressured incident forces rapid decisions across all three dimensions at once.

Or selects the correct terrain type and capacity but inadvertently includes a resource still on cooldown, leaving actual dispatch capacity below threshold. 'Close' selections that satisfy two of the three constraints but fail on the third are the signature pattern.

Separately, cooldown-violation rate (selecting a resource still in cooldown) spikes to ≥ 40% on rounds immediately following a high-severity incident. Errors cluster on incidents where at least two constraints are non-trivial simultaneously.
revised_entry.best_feedback_response: Check three things in order: first, does the terrain match? Then, do the capacity numbers add up? Finally, are those resources off cooldown? One step at a time.
revised_entry.best_clean_replay_task: Run a single-constraint drill sequence: (1) terrain-only matching for 2 orders with capacity and cooldown hidden, (2) add capacity matching for 2 orders with cooldown hidden, (3) reintroduce cooldown indicators with an explicit step-by-step checklist overlay (terrain → capacity → cooldown) visible in the UI before each dispatch.
revised_entry.reflection_prompt: When you were picking trucks and helicopters, what did you think about first — the type of terrain, how much water you needed, or which vehicles were available? What order would make it easiest to check all three things?

review_notes: Generated by Misconception Architect for job 'fire-dispatch-pass2-changed'. 3 primary entries revised and passed quality checks. Review the field_diff for each entry before applying.
